$schema: This is descriptive research evidence, not normative doctrine
note: Thematic uses of color across cinema with examples and contradictions. All examples are illustrative only and do NOT represent Prisma color assignments.

color_themes.description: Red frequently associated with intense emotions, violence, passion, and life force across multiple cinematic traditions.
examples: Spirited Away - red signifying spirit world, danger, and transformation
Red Sorghum - red symbolizing vitality, violence, and peasantry
Devdas - opulent reds symbolizing crushing weight of tradition
Black Friday - deep red for aftermath of violence (social wounds)
contradictions: Context-dependent meanings vary significantly across cultures
Can represent both positive (vitality) and negative (violence) concepts
Western vs. Eastern symbolic associations may differ
cultural_variations: Chinese cinema: red as cultural and historical marker
Bollywood: red as emotional intensity and tradition
Western cinema: red as danger and passion

color_themes.description: Blue often used to represent sadness, isolation, alienation, and emotional distance, though context significantly affects meaning.
examples: Black Friday - blue for conspiracy (cold detachment)
Hero - blue sequences representing different emotional states
Early cinema tinting - blue for night scenes
Urban neon aesthetics - blue as modern alienation
contradictions: Can also represent freedom and transcendence (Ponyo - deep blues for ocean freedom)
Technical vs. emotional uses (blue tinting for night vs. blue for sadness)
Cultural context heavily influences interpretation
cultural_variations: Technical tradition: blue for night/cold
Psychological use: blue for isolation/melancholy
Spiritual use: blue for transcendence/infinity

color_themes.description: Green shows strong duality - representing both natural harmony and unnatural corruption/toxicity depending on context and saturation.
examples: Studio Ghibli - 'Ghibli greens' representing nature, restoration, and balance
Vertigo - 'uncanny green' representing ghostly presence and obsession
Amélie - greens as part of warm, natural palette
Contemporary neon noir - acid greens as toxic/unnatural
contradictions: Natural vs. artificial green carry opposite meanings
Saturation and context dramatically affect interpretation
Cultural associations with nature vs. technology vary
cultural_variations: Environmental cinema: green as positive natural force
Horror/thriller: green as unnatural/toxic
Urban cinema: green fluorescent as alienating

color_themes.description: Yellow demonstrates strong contextual duality - can represent joy and optimism or sickness and decay depending on saturation and cultural context.
examples: Studio Ghibli - warm yellows and pastels for nostalgia and comfort
Amélie - yellows as part of optimistic, warm palette
Black Friday - warm yellow for interrogation (tension/sickness)
Early cinema - yellow tinting for day/interior scenes
contradictions: Warm vs. sickly yellow have opposite emotional effects
Cultural associations vary significantly
Technical vs. emotional uses create different meanings
cultural_variations: Western: yellow as happiness/optimism or caution/sickness
Asian cinema: yellow as earth/tradition/imperial power
Technical tradition: yellow for daylight/warmth

color_themes.description: Use of single dominant colors or black-and-white for narrative structure and emotional emphasis.
examples: Hero - each story segment dominated by single color representing different emotional states
Schindler's List - selective color use for narrative emphasis
Contemporary B&W films - artistic choice in color era
Giallo films - monochromatic lighting schemes for psychological effect
contradictions: Monochrome can represent both realism (documentary) and artifice (artistic choice)
Historical vs. contemporary monochrome carry different meanings
Cultural context affects interpretation of color absence
cultural_variations: Art cinema: monochrome as formal choice
Genre cinema: monochrome for psychological effect
Historical cinema: monochrome as period marker

color_themes.description: Distinction between colors that appear natural/organic versus those that are clearly artificial/technological, often carrying different narrative meanings.
examples: Technicolor artificiality vs. Eastmancolor naturalism
Neon lighting as urban alienation marker
Digital color grading creating impossible natural looks
Practical lighting vs. artificial color timing
contradictions: Artificial can represent both spectacle/magic and alienation/corruption
Natural can represent both authenticity and limitation
Technology changes what appears natural vs. artificial
cultural_variations: Hollywood: artificial color as spectacle and production value
Art cinema: artificial color as formal experimentation
Contemporary: digital artificial as creative tool or aesthetic problem

color_themes.description: Use of color to indicate time periods, memory states, or narrative temporal shifts.
examples: The Wizard of Oz - sepia Kansas vs. Technicolor Oz
The Aviator - film stock evolution as biographical progression
Amélie - storybook colors as nostalgic/fantastical time
O Brother Where Art Thou - digital sepia for historical period
contradictions: Same color techniques can represent past or future depending on context
Memory vs. reality color coding varies by filmmaker
Historical accuracy vs. emotional accuracy in period color
cultural_variations: Western cinema: sepia for historical past
Art cinema: color shifts for psychological time
Genre cinema: color coding for narrative structure

color_themes.description: Use of color saturation, palette, and coordination to indicate social class, wealth, and power dynamics.
examples: Devdas - opulent reds and golds symbolizing wealth and tradition
Parasite - color coding for class levels and social spaces
Bollywood maximalism vs. art cinema restraint
Technicolor as premium/prestige vs. Eastmancolor as democratic
contradictions: Rich colors can represent both positive luxury and negative excess
Restrained palettes can indicate both poverty and artistic sophistication
Cultural context heavily affects class-color associations
cultural_variations: Bollywood: saturation as emotional and social intensity
European art cinema: restraint as sophistication
Hollywood: production value as class marker

color_themes.description: Distinction between color approaches for urban (artificial, neon, mixed lighting) versus rural (natural, earth tones, organic) environments.
examples: Hong Kong neon city aesthetic - mixed artificial urban lighting
Studio Ghibli rural environments - natural greens and earth tones
Contemporary neon noir - toxic urban color palettes
Western films - earth tones and natural lighting
contradictions: Urban can represent both excitement and alienation
Rural can represent both authenticity and limitation
Technology changes urban/rural color distinctions
cultural_variations: Asian cinema: urban neon as modernity marker
American cinema: rural earth tones as authenticity
European cinema: urban/rural as class distinction

color_themes.description: Established color associations with specific film genres, though these conventions are frequently subverted.
examples: Horror - desaturated, green/blue tints for unnatural atmosphere
Romance - warm, golden tones for intimacy
Sci-fi - cool blues and artificial lighting for futuristic feel
Western - earth tones and natural lighting for authenticity
contradictions: Genre conventions frequently subverted for artistic effect
Same colors can serve different genres in different contexts
Evolution of technology changes genre color possibilities
cultural_variations: Hollywood genre conventions vs. international variations
Art cinema subversion of genre color codes
Contemporary mixing of genre color languages

extraction_notes.methodology: Themes extracted from patterns observed across multiple films and movements in source document
extraction_notes.ambiguity_preservation: Contradictions and cultural variations maintained rather than resolved
extraction_notes.scope: Covers major thematic uses documented in academic and critical sources
extraction_notes.cultural_sensitivity: Attempts to acknowledge cultural variations in color meaning
